name: wxPython (Cross-Platform GUI with Native Look and Feel)

performance.notes: Native OS widgets — best native look and feel among Python GUI toolkits

agent_readiness.notes: Native OS widget toolkit using actual OS controls. import wx. app = wx.App(). frame = wx.Frame(None, title='Hello'). panel = wx.Panel(frame). btn = wx.Button(panel, label='Click Me'). btn.Bind(wx.EVT_BUTTON, handler). frame.Show(). app.MainLoop(). Uses native Win32/Cocoa/GTK widgets. wx.Grid for tables. wx.ArtProvider for system icons. wx.aui for docking layouts. GridSizer/BoxSizer/FlexGridSizer for layouts. Mature (20+ years). Largest widget set after Qt. Verbosity of API is higher than Qt.

gotchas: Verbose API with C++ origins — less Pythonic than tkinter or Kivy
Installation can be complex — large binary wheel (~25MB)
Platform-specific behavior can cause cross-platform inconsistencies
Less active development than Qt — fewer new features
Documentation quality is inconsistent across modules
best_when: Applications requiring truly native OS look and feel
Windows desktop applications where native controls are required
Long-running applications where native widget behavior matters
When large widget set (grid, tree, rich text) is needed without Qt complexity
avoid_when: New cross-platform projects (Qt/PySide6 is better maintained)
When Qt's comprehensive ecosystem is preferable
summary: wxPython uses native OS widgets (Win32/Cocoa/GTK) for the most native look and feel of any Python GUI. wx.Frame + wx.Panel + wx.Button with wx.EVT_BUTTON event binding. Sizer-based layout. Grid, tree, rich text, AUI docking. 20+ year track record. wxWindows LGPL license. Verbosity is high vs Qt. Best when native OS widget behavior is critical; for new projects PySide6 has better maintenance and ecosystem.
